architecture.summary: VisualPRM is an 8B-parameter multimodal Process Reward Model that scores step-by-step reasoning chains from MLLMs. It takes an image, question, and solution steps as multi-turn chat input and predicts correctness of each step. During Best-of-N evaluation, it ranks multiple candidate responses from a policy MLLM and selects the highest-scored one. The model uses a value-based approach where each step's quality is determined by its expected accuracy (computed during training via Monte Carlo sampling), formulated as multi-turn dialogue to leverage MLLM generation capacity.
trained_components: VisualPRM (8B multimodal model trained on VisualPRM400K)

biblio.title: VisualPRM: An Effective Process Reward Model for Multimodal Reasoning

claims.dataset: seven multimodal reasoning benchmarks (MMMU, MathVista, MathVision, MathVerse-VO, DynaMath, WeMath, LogicVista)
claims.statement: VisualPRM achieves a 5.9-point improvement across seven multimodal reasoning benchmarks when applied to InternVL2.5-78B

VisualPRM improves the overall reasoning performance of MiniCPM-V2.6, QwenVL2.5-7B, InternVL2.5-8B, and InternVL2.5-78B by 8.0, 3.7, 8.4, and 5.9 points, respectively, across seven multimodal reasoning benchmarks
VisualPRM achieves an overall macro F1 score of 62.0 on VisualProcessBench for detecting erroneous steps, outperforming GPT-4o (60.3) and performing on par with Gemini-2.0-Flash (62.3)
VisualPRM outperforms Self-Consistency and Outcome Reward Models in Best-of-8 evaluation, with PRM outperforming SC and ORM by 2.4 and 1.5 points respectively on InternVL2.5-8B

VisualPRM400K dataset comprises approximately 400K samples with 2 million steps of process supervision, averaging 5.6 steps per response and 22.6 words per step, with approximately 10% incorrect steps

VisualProcessBench comprises 2,866 samples with 26,950 human-annotated step-wise correctness labels across five multimodal reasoning benchmarks, with 16,585 correct steps, 7,691 incorrect steps, and 2,674 neutral steps

claims.dataset: seven multimodal reasoning benchmarks and VisualProcessBench
claims.statement: Value-based PRMs outperform advantage-based PRMs; supervising all steps (w/o early stop) yields better performance than supervising only up to the first incorrect step during training

Existing open-source MLLMs struggle to accurately assess the correctness of each step as critic models, with most achieving F1 scores close to random guessing baseline of 50.0, tending to provide positive analysis and label most steps as correct
